channel.title: Comments for page "Forum Post"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Forum Post"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
100% agree. Doesn't stop me from enjoying Broken Masquerade, but the setup is so OTT and it would probably be a bit more grounded and intriguing if the readers and contributors were able to play with the mystery of why the masquerade failed.

BTW, I associate you so strongly with this canon, I often forget you didn't create it. Consider that a compliment!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, fair, and I didn't phrase that the best. By "inherently part of the setting and not even explained" I meant "no one explains how the secrecy was blown, we just know that it was." But maybe that'd be a cop-out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually enjoy the setting even more for the sudden nature of the Broken Masquerade. Would a Foundation that had always been in the open have been as cold and secretive as the Foundation we're all familiar with? And what is the Foundation in the absence of dying in the dark or "shut up and calculate" ethics?

The premise of Broken Masquerade isn't so much "what if people knew about the Foundation?" as it is "What if everything went to hell and it all came to light?", which I find much more compelling. Certainly an "Absent Masquerade" canon would be interesting, but probably more bureaucratic and Kafkaesque than Broken Masquerade is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thank you for the encouraging feedback!

the concept has my suspension of disbelief hanging by its fingernails

I somewhat agree with this. I think I'd find it easier to work with this canon's setting if the major difference in it (the Foundation's publicity) were not the result of a freak accident, but rather inherently part of the setting and not even explained. But I work with what I got since I wasn't around in 2013, so I won't complain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I always dig your stuff Cyantreuse. Which is funny with Broken Masquerade, because I don't want to like it (just because the concept has my suspension of disbelief hanging by its fingernails), but you always make it so compelling and real. This was no exception; I found myself wanting to get dragged into the (fictional) arguments in the (fictional) comments, so well done for making me facepalm myself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Soldiers absolutely do comment on the Army's social media (and the PR units are all soldiers too, FWIW).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm just struggling to think of any comparable real-world organisation where you can interact with the employees in this relatively unregimented way (at least where they identify themselves as employees). I guess it's low down on the list of unrealistic things the Foundations does, though.

I had convinced myself this was about mental health (de)institutionalisation, maybe because I remembered the seized items tale (I didn't realise you had written both until I just checked, though).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

I don't see it as social media, more an information and discussion page/site. The concept I was more aiming for is that Foundation employees are expected to be mature and reasonable enough to interact with civilians appropriately, and that extends to online. There's also no obligation to post there, and I'm sure that's why very few Foundation personnel actually posted in it, and none of them argued with civilians unless to clarify a fact. While I can see the reasoning behind saying they wouldn't have this system at all, if they do have it, I don't see why it can't function somewhat professionally. Anyone whose job is to protect society should at least be able to interact with society online in a levelheaded manner. If they don't, there are moderators on IntSCPFN watching their behavior and reporting it to supervisors if necessary. It's not a place where you can just say whatever without consequence, and personnel know that.

hmm i wonder what real life situation this might be an allegory for???

It's not anything specific, at least that I intended. If you mean "is it supposed to look like 4chan boards," it's not what crossed my mind while I was writing but I can see the connection because of having anons and a simple text format. If you mean the issues the skip is addressing, this one wasn't inspired by real-world issues other than politics (and voting season) in general, unlike two other tales for this canon that you might be referencing. The specific issue of containing humans isn't allegory for anything in this case.

I'm glad that you liked it despite the issues, though!

Edit: I agree that Rogers was toeing the behavior line and not a good example. I changed his comment and Trauss' reply accordingly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do these sites not have a social media team though? All the posts in this story are made within a few hours so unless your team was gigantic only one person would be responding to them.

In any case, the Foundation is more like a military/ research institution, not a video game company, and those kinds of organisations have PR teams, they don't have their soldiers or scientists commenting on their social media.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Having a single designated social media guy for a toxic community (whether it be League of Legends, Overwatch, Runescape or the Broken Masquerade-verse) is a mistake, because 1) all fuck-ups are going to be blamed on them 2) they're going to get the brunt of the hatred from everyone on the sub and get them frustrated and 3) that frustration is going to lead to the social media guy blowing up and making everyone involved look like asses.

When game devs to post on the Overwatch or League or RS subreddits, it's usually in turns. You can tell that being yelled at by people with the maturity of twelve-year-olds when it comes to a game they allegedly love gets to them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
hmm i wonder what real life situation this might be an allegory for???

In all seriousness, this is a well-crafted, realistic story in a fairly novel but accessible format, +1.

The number of different Foundation staff who have the time (or interest) to argue with internet anons is a little implausible though, as opposed to having a single social media guy - as is staff member Rogers taking shots at a colleague on a public forum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nicely handled!

"Oh, you think WE'VE been unethical?! Spend a fortnight in the driver's seat and get back to me."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had the upvote pretty much sealed by the end of the first paragraph, and I never stopped loving this. As always I really enjoy the nuance you bring to the Foundation's entire deal.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Despite my normally negative viewpoint of BM, I actually liked this quite a bit and upvoted half way through. Plus a cool CSS will always make me like something

+1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
I guess this is kind of a format experiment. It was a little difficult to balance having a narrative structure (the typical exposition-to-resolution timeline) with the inherently chaotic structure of conversations in a web forum, but it is what it is, I rearranged the comments several times until I thought they were in the right places for narrative layout, and now I'm coldposting it. ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

It's definitely a short story, which is something I typically have trouble producing, as we have seen from most of my long-ass works in this canon alone as well as elsewhere.

Also… related to the subject matter… Hey kids, IRL talk here, did you vote? You can vote early you know. Voting is important; please do it!
